description: A measurement of the total amount of cholesterol in the blood, representing the sum of cholesterol carried in various lipoprotein particles including LDL, HDL, and VLDL.

Triglycerides are the most common type of fat (lipid) in the body, used for energy storage. High levels in the blood are associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease and acute pancreatitis.

aliases: Colesterol de lipoproteína de alta densidad
description: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol measures the cholesterol carried by HDL particles, which absorb cholesterol in the blood and carry it back to the liver to be flushed from the body. Higher levels are associated with a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease.

Cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprotein (LDL) particles. LDL is often called 'bad' cholesterol because high levels lead to the buildup of plaque in arteries (atherosclerosis), increasing the risk of heart disease and stroke.

Very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) cholesterol is a component of total cholesterol. VLDL particles primarily carry triglycerides throughout the body; high levels are associated with an increased risk of plaque buildup in the arteries and cardiovascular disease.

Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is an enzyme found primarily in the liver. It is released into the bloodstream when liver cells are damaged or inflamed, making it a highly specific biomarker for liver injury.

An enzyme primarily found in the liver, heart, and muscles. Elevated levels in the blood typically indicate cellular damage in these tissues, most commonly associated with liver injury or myocardial infarction.
An enzyme found in high concentrations in the liver, bile ducts, and bones. It is primarily used to screen for or monitor liver disease or bone disorders.

The measure of the total amount of testosterone in the blood, including the portion bound to proteins (SHBG and albumin) and the free portion. It is the primary sex hormone in males and is present in smaller amounts in females.

Apolipoprotein A-1 is the primary protein component of high-density lipoprotein (HDL) particles. It plays a critical role in reverse cholesterol transport, helping to clear excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and transporting it to the liver for excretion.

Apolipoprotein B is the primary structural protein found on the surface of all potentially atherogenic lipoprotein particles, including VLDL, IDL, and LDL. It is considered a highly accurate marker for cardiovascular risk as it represents the total number of atherogenic particles in circulation.

The most potent and prevalent form of estrogen in the body, primarily produced by the ovaries in females and the testes and adrenal glands in males. It is critical for reproductive health, bone density, and cardiovascular function.
An essential trace element that serves as a cofactor for antioxidant enzymes like glutathione peroxidase and is vital for thyroid hormone metabolism and immune function.

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) is an essential omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid that must be obtained through the diet. It serves as a precursor for the synthesis of longer-chain omega-3 fatty acids, eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA).

An essential omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) that must be obtained through the diet. It serves as a structural component of cell membranes and is the metabolic precursor to arachidonic acid and other eicosanoids.

An essential long-chain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid primarily obtained from marine sources like fatty fish. It serves as a precursor for anti-inflammatory eicosanoids and is clinically monitored for cardiovascular risk reduction and the management of severe hypertriglyceridemia.

Measures the total concentration of 25-hydroxyvitamin D (D2 and D3), the primary circulating form of vitamin D. It is the gold standard for assessing vitamin D sufficiency, reflecting intake from diet, supplements, and sun exposure.
An essential trace mineral required for the catalytic activity of approximately 100 enzymes, immune function, protein synthesis, wound healing, and DNA synthesis.

An essential omega-6 fatty acid derived from gamma-linolenic acid (GLA). It acts as a precursor to series 1 prostaglandins (PGE1), which have anti-inflammatory properties, and can also be converted into arachidonic acid.
Free triiodothyronine (FT3) measures the unbound, biologically active form of the T3 hormone, which regulates metabolism, body temperature, and heart rate.
A water-soluble vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, neurological function, and the formation of red blood cells. It acts as a cofactor for methionine synthase and L-methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.
Free T4 (thyroxine) is the metabolically active, unbound fraction of the primary hormone produced by the thyroid gland. It is used to assess thyroid function and diagnose hyper- or hypothyroidism.
A blood protein that stores iron in the body's cells. It is the primary diagnostic marker for total body iron stores.

The sum of the concentrations of essential omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids in the blood, primarily including alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosapentaenoic acid (DPA), and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA).

An omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid (18:3n-6) synthesized from linoleic acid. It serves as a precursor to dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid (DGLA), which can be converted into anti-inflammatory eicosanoids.

Total calcium measures the concentration of both free (ionized) calcium and calcium bound to proteins (primarily albumin) and anions in the blood. It is a vital indicator of bone health, parathyroid function, and kidney health.
A measurement of the amount of iron in the liquid portion of the blood (serum) that is bound to transferrin.
An essential omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid that is a primary structural component of the human brain, cerebral cortex, skin, and retina.

The total concentration of omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids in the blood, primarily composed of linoleic acid (LA), arachidonic acid (AA), gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), and dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid (DGLA).

Oleic acid is the most abundant monounsaturated omega-9 fatty acid in human circulation. Primarily obtained through dietary sources like olive oil or synthesized from stearic acid, it is a major component of membrane phospholipids and triglycerides. It is associated with improved cardiovascular health, reduced inflammation, and maintenance of cell membrane fluidity.

The sum of all polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) in the blood, comprising both the omega-3 (e.g., ALA, EPA, DHA) and omega-6 (e.g., LA, ARA) series. This measurement reflects dietary fat quality, essential fatty acid status, and systemic lipid metabolism.
A glycoprotein hormone produced by the anterior pituitary gland that regulates the endocrine function of the thyroid gland, stimulating the production of thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3).

An essential omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid that serves as a key precursor for the synthesis of eicosanoids, including prostaglandins and leukotrienes, which mediate inflammatory and physiological processes.
An enzyme found in high concentrations in the liver and bile ducts. It is a sensitive marker for detecting liver injury, biliary obstruction, and chronic alcohol consumption.
A measure of the average size (volume) of a single red blood cell, primarily used to classify anemias as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic.
A waste product created when the body breaks down purines, which are found in some foods and are also made by the body. High levels can lead to gout or kidney stones.
The primary sugar found in the blood and the body's main source of energy. Fasting glucose levels are used to screen for and monitor prediabetes and diabetes mellitus.
A measure of the average concentration of hemoglobin within a single red blood cell relative to the volume of the cell.
A genetic variant of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) containing apolipoprotein(a), recognized as an independent, inherited risk factor for cardiovascular disease and aortic stenosis.
A quantitative measurement of the number of platelets (thrombocytes) per liter of blood, used to evaluate the body's ability to form blood clots and maintain hemostasis.
Sodium is the primary extracellular cation and is essential for maintaining fluid balance, osmotic pressure, and proper nerve and muscle function.
Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) measures the average blood glucose levels over the past 2-3 months by determining the percentage of hemoglobin in red blood cells that has glucose attached to it.

Total bilirubin is a measurement of the total amount of bilirubin in the blood, representing the sum of conjugated (direct) and unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin. It is used to evaluate liver function, bile duct patency, and red blood cell breakdown.
The total number of white blood cells (leukocytes) in a volume of blood, used to evaluate immune system function and identify infection, inflammation, or bone marrow disorders.

aliases: Capacidad total de fijación de hierro
Capacité totale de fixation du fer
Capacità totale di legame del ferro
description: A measurement of the maximum amount of iron that can be bound by proteins in the blood, primarily transferrin. It is used to evaluate iron metabolism and diagnose conditions like iron-deficiency anemia or hemochromatosis.

A low-molecular-weight protein produced by all nucleated cells at a constant rate. It is filtered by the glomerulus and not reabsorbed into the bloodstream, making it a sensitive marker for estimating glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) and kidney function, independent of muscle mass.
A type of white blood cell (leukocyte) involved in the immune response to parasitic infections, allergic reactions, and certain inflammatory conditions.
The percentage of eosinophils among circulating white blood cells in a differential blood count.
Potassium is an essential intracellular electrolyte and mineral critical for nerve impulse transmission, muscle contraction, and the maintenance of normal heart rhythm and fluid balance.
Epithelial cells identified in urine sediment during microscopic examination. They include squamous, transitional, and renal tubular types.
A measurement of the average amount of hemoglobin contained within a single red blood cell.
A nitrogenous byproduct of protein metabolism formed in the liver and excreted by the kidneys. It is a key marker of renal function and protein breakdown.
Inorganic phosphate (measured as phosphorus) is a vital mineral used by the body for energy production (ATP), bone and teeth formation, and cell membrane integrity.
The iron-containing protein in red blood cells responsible for transporting oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.
A type of white blood cell (granulocyte) involved in the immune response to allergens and parasites, responsible for releasing inflammatory mediators like histamine.
The percentage of basophils among circulating white blood cells in a differential blood count.
The proportion of the total volume of blood that is occupied by red blood cells. It is a key indicator of hydration status and red blood cell production or loss.

Globulins are a group of proteins in the blood that include enzymes, antibodies, and more than 500 other proteins. They are categorized into alpha, beta, and gamma globulins and are vital for liver function, blood clotting, and immune response.
A high-sensitivity measure of C-reactive protein used to evaluate low-grade systemic inflammation and assess risk for cardiovascular disease.

Non-HDL cholesterol (Non-HDL-C) represents the sum of cholesterol carried by all atherogenic, apolipoprotein B-containing lipoproteins, including LDL, VLDL, IDL, and Lp(a). It is clinically used as a robust predictor of cardiovascular risk and is typically calculated as Total Cholesterol minus HDL Cholesterol, though direct assays also exist.

A subset of white blood cells (leukocytes) that play a central role in the immune system, including T cells, B cells, and natural killer cells, responsible for identifying and eliminating pathogens and abnormal cells.
The percentage of lymphocytes among circulating white blood cells in a differential blood count.
An amino acid produced during the metabolism of methionine. Elevated levels are an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease, venous thrombosis, and neurodegenerative conditions.

Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell (leukocyte) and serve as the first line of defense in the innate immune system, primarily responding to bacterial infections and tissue injury through phagocytosis and the release of antimicrobial enzymes.
The percentage of neutrophils among circulating white blood cells in a differential blood count.

A yellow-orange pigment formed during the normal breakdown of red blood cells. It is processed by the liver and eventually excreted in the bile and stool. Total bilirubin levels represent the sum of conjugated (direct) and unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin.
Qualitative urine bilirubin result from dipstick or strip testing.

A glycoprotein enzyme produced by the epithelial cells of the prostate gland. It is used as a clinical marker for prostate cancer and other prostatic conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).
Folate is a B-vitamin (B9) essential for DNA synthesis, repair, and methylation, as well as the production and maintenance of new cells, particularly red blood cells.
A count of the actual number of red blood cells per volume of blood, used to evaluate the blood's capacity to transport oxygen to tissues.

Albumin is the most abundant protein in human plasma, synthesized by the liver. It maintains osmotic pressure to prevent fluid leakage from blood vessels and transports various hormones, vitamins, and drugs throughout the body.
A waste product produced by the normal breakdown of muscle tissue. It is filtered from the blood by the kidneys and excreted in the urine, making it a key indicator of renal function.

Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cell (leukocyte) in the human body. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating into tissues to differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells, where they play a primary role in the innate immune response, phagocytosis, and the initiation of adaptive immunity.
The percentage of monocytes among circulating white blood cells in a differential blood count.

A calculation that estimates how well the kidneys filter waste from the blood, normalized to a standard body surface area of 1.73 square meters. It is the primary clinical tool for diagnosing and staging chronic kidney disease (CKD).
A measurement of the combined amount of albumin and globulins in the serum or plasma. It is used to assess nutritional status and screen for disorders of the liver, kidneys, or bone marrow.
A steroid hormone (glucocorticoid) produced by the adrenal cortex that regulates metabolism, immune response, and the body's response to stress. It exhibits a significant diurnal rhythm.

A colorless byproduct of bilirubin reduction formed in the intestines by bacterial action. Most urobilinogen is excreted in feces, but approximately 10-15% is reabsorbed into the portal circulation and subsequently excreted by the kidneys.
A peptide hormone produced by the beta cells of the pancreatic islets that regulates carbohydrate and fat metabolism by promoting the absorption of glucose from the blood.
A measure of the variation in red blood cell volume (size) and a standard component of a complete blood count (CBC). Higher values indicate greater variation in cell size, known as anisocytosis.
Chloride is an essential electrolyte and the major extracellular anion. It works alongside sodium and potassium to maintain osmotic pressure, fluid balance, and acid-base status (pH) in the body.
A measurement of the acidity or alkalinity of the blood, representing the concentration of hydrogen ions. It is critical for maintaining homeostatic enzymatic and metabolic functions.
Urine pH from urinalysis or dipstick testing.

A hormone similar in molecular structure to insulin which plays an important role in childhood growth and continues to have anabolic effects in adults. It is primarily produced by the liver in response to growth hormone (GH) stimulation.

Detects the presence of intact red blood cells (hematuria) or free hemoglobin (hemoglobinuria) in the urine. A positive result may indicate kidney stones, infection, trauma, or underlying renal disease.
The percentage of transferrin that is saturated with iron, calculated by dividing the serum iron level by the total iron-binding capacity (TIBC).

aliases: Homeostatic Model Assessment of Beta-cell Function
description: An index derived from fasting insulin and glucose levels used to estimate the steady-state functional capacity of pancreatic beta cells. A value of 100% represents the reference beta-cell function of a healthy young adult.

label_map.high: Increased beta-cell function (often compensatory for insulin resistance)

description: The ratio of albumin to globulin proteins in the blood. It is used to help evaluate health conditions involving the liver, kidneys, and immune system.

label_map.low: Low (may indicate liver disease, kidney disease, or inflammation/infection)
label_map.high: High (may indicate certain types of leukemia or genetic disorders)
